evals.prompt: Set up Detox in our React Native app for iOS and Android. We want simulator/emulator runs locally and headless CI runs on GitHub Actions.
evals.expected_output: Should check qa-context.md or ask. Should run `npx detox init`, edit `.detoxrc.js` with both ios.debug and android.debug app configs (build commands for Xcode and Gradle), simulator and emulator devices, and ios.sim.debug + android.emu.debug configurations. Should mention adding testID props to components for stable selection, splitting iOS and Android into separate CI jobs (macOS for iOS, Linux for Android), and using `--headless` in CI. Should pin Detox and RN versions. Should NOT invent .detoxrc keys.
assertions: Reads qa-context.md or asks for it
Names both iOS and Android app configs with build commands
Mentions testID props for stable selection
Splits iOS and Android into separate CI jobs

evals.prompt: Our Detox test does `await element(by.id('x')).tap()` then immediately `expect(element(by.id('result'))).toBeVisible()`. Sometimes the result isn't there yet. Why doesn't Detox wait?
evals.expected_output: Should explain that Detox already waits for the JS bridge / main thread / animations / pending fetches via XMLHttpRequest. If the app uses something Detox doesn't see (custom native modules, raw sockets, deferred work), Detox can't sync on it. Fix: use `waitFor(element(by.id('result'))).toBeVisible().withTimeout(5000)`. Should warn against `sleep` and recommend identifying which async surface isn't being synced — if it's a custom module, file an issue or wrap the work in something Detox recognizes. Should explain that `waitFor` is the explicit-wait equivalent.
assertions: Identifies unhooked async work as the cause
Suggests reporting / wrapping unsynced async surfaces

evals.prompt: how do i scroll a list to find an element that isn't visible yet
evals.expected_output: Should treat the casual phrasing as a valid trigger. Should introduce the `whileElement` pattern: `await waitFor(element(by.id('target'))).toBeVisible().whileElement(by.id('list')).scroll(200, 'down')`. Should explain this scrolls 200 points down at a time and stops when the target becomes visible or the timeout hits. Should also mention `scrollTo('bottom')` for simple cases where the target is the last item. Should NOT invent action methods.

evals.prompt: Our Detox suite leaks state between tests — User A's profile shows up in User B's test. How do we isolate?
evals.expected_output: Should explain the isolation primitives: (1) `device.reloadReactNative()` in `beforeEach` — fast, resets JS state but keeps native services; (2) `device.launchApp({ newInstance: true, ... })` — full restart, slower but clean; (3) app-level state reset via launchArgs (the app sees `detoxTesting: true` and clears AsyncStorage / DB before bootstrap); (4) per-test mock data with unique IDs so even shared state doesn't collide. Should recommend reloadReactNative for most tests, launchApp for ones that need a full cold start, and app-side cleanup hooks for stubborn state. Should NOT recommend just hoping for the best.
assertions: Names reloadReactNative and launchApp({newInstance: true}) explicitly
Recommends unique IDs per test as a defense
Acknowledges trade-off between cleanup completeness and speed
Does not invent Detox lifecycle hooks

evals.prompt: We have both Detox and Maestro. When should we pick which?
evals.expected_output: Should frame as a trade-off. Detox: gray-box sync, JS-native test code, Jest runner integration, deep RN awareness, full code logic in tests. Maestro: YAML flows, simpler syntax, faster to write basic flows, less powerful for branching logic / data-driven tests, single source for iOS and Android. Recommend: Detox for the regression suite owned by engineers; Maestro for fast smoke flows or QA-led exploratory automation. Defer to qa-context.md (team makeup, complexity of test logic, parallelization budget). Cross-references maestro.
assertions: Frames as trade-off, not a winner
Recommends Maestro for QA-led or smoke flows

evals.prompt: Our Detox build takes 8 minutes on every PR. How do we speed up CI?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose and recommend: (1) cache the build output between PRs that don't touch native code — split `detox build` (cached) from `detox test` (always runs); (2) only rebuild when iOS / Android folders or relevant configs change; (3) use Detox in release configuration only when needed (debug builds for fast iteration); (4) split iOS and Android into parallel jobs; (5) for the test phase, parallelize via Jest's test sharding across multiple runners. Should warn against caching the simulator/emulator binaries themselves (large, OS-specific). Cross-reference ci-test-orchestration.
assertions: Recommends caching the Detox build output
Recommends conditional rebuild when native folders change
Suggests Jest sharding for test phase parallelism
